ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for seaweed-wrapped fried chicken; closest defensible match is fried chicken food product (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (chicken meat, coated and fried) is the closest defensible match; no specific code exists for seaweed-wrapped fried chicken.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for seaweed chicken; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of seaweed chicken in Singapore is approximately 150g, equivalent to two to three pieces of deep-fried chicken wrapped in seaweed, as commonly served at hawker centres and zi char stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Seaweed Chicken — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Seaweed Chicken (CR ID: F11070005). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on battered/coated fried chicken with mixed carbohydrate coating
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seaweed chicken. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the closest analogous food: battered and fried chicken with a mixed wheat-flour coating, as referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database. The seaweed wrapping and moderate carbohydrate content (11.6g/100g) are consistent with a low GI profile. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (45 × 11.6 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.8g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 725mg per 100g — exceeds the 600mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit seaweed chicken due to its very high sodium content of 725mg/100g; if consumed, portion should not exceed 80g per meal and low-sodium soy sauce should be requested.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (11.6g/100g) partially offset by dietary fibre (2.3g/100g); deep-frying increases caloric density and saturated fat load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Seaweed chicken is acceptable in moderate portions for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given its low GI (~45), high protein (17.7g/100g), and fibre content, but the deep-fried preparation increases caloric density and may worsen insulin resistance if consumed frequently; limit to one serving of 100–120g and avoid sweet dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (725mg/100g) and deep-fried preparation contributing saturated fat and potential trans fat from repeated frying oil use
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Seaweed chicken poses a high cardiovascular risk due to its elevated sodium content (725mg/100g) and deep-fried preparation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish or request a baked alternative with reduced soy sauce seasoning.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seaweed Chicken (海苔鸡) is a popular Singaporean zi char and hawker dish consisting of bite-sized chicken pieces wrapped in dried seaweed (nori) and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The seaweed imparts a savoury, umami-rich flavour to the juicy chicken interior, while the batter creates a light, crisp exterior. The dish is typically seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, and Chinese five-spice or garlic before frying. Per 100g, it provides 238 kcal, 17.7g protein, 13.5g fat, and 11.6g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of 725mg. The dietary fibre contribution of 2.3g comes primarily from the seaweed. Clinically, the high sodium level (725mg/100g) makes this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant portion restriction. The low glycemic index (~45) and high protein content make it relatively acceptable for Type 2 Diabetes management in controlled portions. The deep-frying method generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which may worsen inflammation in conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis. This dish is commonly served as a side dish or shared plate at hawker centres, zi char restaurants, and coffee shops across Singapore, and is widely available as a takeaway option.
common_user_queries: How many calories in seaweed chicken?
Is seaweed chicken high in sodium?
What is seaweed chicken made of?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID: F11070005) with a confidence of 0.95. This is the canonical primary source for Singapore food composition data. The record is marked as reviewed with provenance canonical. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, which slightly reduces overall confidence for the GI component only.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 3.5g/100g based on typical deep-fried battered chicken composition from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, fried, batter); this field was not reported in the HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this food item, and no supplementary approved source was available to fill these fields without risking unsupported estimation. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on battered and fried chicken; no direct GI measurement for seaweed chicken was found in any approved source. Glycemic load per 150g serving calculated as (45 × 11.6 × 1.5) / 100 = 7.8g.
